title: A realistic toy experiment
learningObjectives: Design a controlled toy experiment to evaluate SetConCA against baselines.
Formulate expected outcomes for feature-to-concept alignment, absorption, and cross-seed matching.
Predict the behavior of the model under specific failure conditions like random grouping or intruder tokens.

whyThisLesson.text:
Before running large-scale distributed training on massive language models, researchers build toy models. A good toy experiment isolate the core mechanism, provides ground-truth answers, and allows for rapid iteration.

In this lesson, we design a realistic toy world where we know the exact ground-truth concepts. We will define the data generation process, the baselines, and the specific metrics we need to measure to prove (or falsify) SetConCA's claims.

sections.bridgeFrom: We have our objective and our strategy for creating sets. Now we need a controlled environment to prove that the combination actually works as hypothesized.

intuition.analogy: Testing a new aerodynamic wing design in a wind tunnel before bolting it to a real airplane.
intuition.analogyLimit: The wind tunnel is perfectly controlled; real LM activations have unknown, nearly infinite dimensions of variation.
intuition.text:
In a real LM, we don't know what the 'true' concepts are, so evaluating an SAE is tricky. We rely on proxies like automated interpretability or downstream steering. 

In a toy experiment, we play God. We create the concepts, we mix them together to create activations, and we add the noise. Because we know the exact ground truth, we can measure exactly how well our SetConCA dictionary recovers the original concepts compared to a baseline SAE.

toyExample.description: A d=8 dimensional activation space with 5 ground-truth concept directions. Activations are sparse linear combinations plus noise.

steps.action: Generate 1000 activations with known concept labels and group them into 200 sets of 5 tokens each (sharing 1-2 concepts).
steps.result: A controlled dataset with ground-truth semantic sets.
steps.insight: This provides the perfect testbed for evaluating the contrastive loss.

steps.action: Train a pointwise TopK SAE (width=16) and a SetConCA model on the sets.
steps.result: Both models yield a dictionary of 16 features.
steps.insight: We can now directly compare the dictionaries.

steps.action: Measure feature-to-concept cosine similarity, absorption rate, and cross-seed matching (3 seeds each).
steps.result: Evaluate the core hypotheses of the SetConCA architecture.
steps.insight: Metrics must be rigorously defined to isolate the effect of the set-level losses.

visual.question: How does SetConCA perform in the controlled toy world?
visual.description: An interactive environment showing the 8D toy space, comparing the feature directions learned by the TopK SAE vs. SetConCA against the ground-truth concept vectors.

technical.text: The experiment requires careful controls. We must test what happens when the assumptions of SetConCA are violated. We will introduce 'intruder tokens' (tokens in a set that don't share the concept), use random groupings, and test zero concept sharing.
definitions: Feature-to-concept cosine similarity: The maximum cosine similarity between a learned dictionary feature and any ground-truth concept direction.
Cross-seed matching: The consistency of the learned dictionary across independent training runs with different random initializations.
Intruder token: A token artificially inserted into a semantic set that does not share the target concept, simulating real-world noise.

mathematics.label: Activation Generation
mathematics.plainExplanation: Each activation is a combination of a few active concepts plus some background noise.
mathematics.whyNeeded: To establish a known ground truth for evaluating recovery.
mathematics.ifRemoved: We wouldn't know if the SAE is learning real concepts or just modeling noise.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That real LMs behave exactly like this linear mixture model.

evidence.claim: SetConCA should show higher feature-to-concept cosine on the SHARED concepts.
evidence.label: [Hypothesis]
evidence.paraphrase: The contrastive loss guides the features closer to the true shared concepts.

evidence.claim: SetConCA should show lower absorption (each feature maps to fewer concepts).
evidence.label: [Hypothesis]

evidence.claim: SetConCA should show higher cross-seed matching.
evidence.label: [Hypothesis]
evidence.paraphrase: The set constraints reduce the number of degenerate local minima, improving stability.

evidence.claim: Worse than baseline when tested with BAD sets (random grouping).
evidence.label: [Hypothesis]
evidence.paraphrase: Random sets destroy the model's ability to learn anything.

evidence.label: [Hypothesis]
evidence.paraphrase: The model gracefully (or catastrophically) degrades as set purity drops.

evidence.claim: SetConCA degrades to pointwise when concept sharing is ZERO.
evidence.label: [Hypothesis]
evidence.paraphrase: Without shared concepts, the contrastive loss provides no useful gradient.

failureModes.name: Intruder Collapse
failureModes.mechanism: The contrastive loss is highly sensitive to outliers, and intruder tokens drag the set representation away from the true concept.
failureModes.symptom: Feature purity drops dramatically even with a small percentage of intruder tokens.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Plot feature-to-concept similarity as a function of intruder percentage.
failureModes.mitigation: Use robust pooling mechanisms (e.g., attention instead of mean) to ignore outliers in the set.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: Crucial for predicting performance on real LM data, which is inherently noisy.

setconcaConnection.relevance: This experiment is the primary empirical proof-of-concept for SetConCA.
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: Testing against standard TopK SAEs in a synthetic setting.
setconcaConnection.proposedExperiment: Execute the defined toy experiment and analyze the results across multiple random seeds.

exercises.prompt: If SetConCA shows high feature-to-concept cosine but VERY high reconstruction error (high FVU), what might be wrong with the hyperparameters?
exercises.reveal: The contrastive weight (β) and alignment weight (γ) are likely too high relative to the reconstruction weight, forcing the model to capture the concept at the expense of reconstructing the individual token's full activation.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Why is it important to test SetConCA with 'zero concept sharing' as a boundary condition?
rubric: Identifies that it tests whether the architecture inherently degrades learning when there is no signal.
Verifies that SetConCA behaves no worse than a baseline SAE when set information is absent.
masteryQuestions.badVersion: To see if it works without concepts.
masteryQuestions.goodVersion: Testing with zero concept sharing acts as a sanity check. If sets share no concepts, the contrastive loss should theoretically provide zero useful signal. If SetConCA performs significantly worse than the baseline SAE in this scenario, it means the architecture itself introduces instability or harmful biases when the set assumptions fail.

bridgeToNext.text: The toy experiment provides the initial evidence. Now, we must formulate a complete research program around these findings, defining the precise hypotheses, baselines, and critical ablations required for a rigorous paper.
